was called for trial you were again


too busy serving your friends, the


police. in the prosecution of Gurley


Flynn for conspiracy, to try the At


kinson case? You assigned the


case to one of your deputies, when


you had promised the grand jury


yourself that you could secure a
! conviction of Atkinson. You know


Mr.. Pugh. that Atkinson has the


backing of the county court house
machine of which you father Felix


Pugh, is the head Is this the reas-
on, Mr.. Pugh, that you were too
| busy trying to convict Gurley
Flynn of the heipous crime of con-
spring to speak on the streets to
give your personal attention to the
prosecution of the case against Cal
| Atkinson?


r. X. PUGH.


Spokane county is paying 80
cents per day for feeding prisoners
at the county Jail. The money is
paid to F. x. Pugh, sheriff, who
serves two meals per day to the
prisoners at an average cost per
meal Of 17h cents per meal. The
county furnishes everything except
| the food, the prisoners do the work,
and in this way Sheriff Pugh last
year collected $4,000 from Spo-
! kane county.


Even if the food were good and
plentiful, the price is out Of reason,
when it is taken into consideration
that the sheriff pays neither heat,
light, rent or help, which forms the
big end of the average restaurant
mans expense. Yet the county


pays as much as an ordinary meal
can be had at some of the cheaper


restaurants of the city.


| Spokane city feeds its prisoners
| for 10 cents per meal, and the man
who has the contract does the cook


ing away from the Jail and furn-
ishes his own light and fuel. pays


his own rent and help and still


makes money.


What is the rake-off of the sher-
| if under the present plan of feed
| ing the prisoners? An ex-sheriff,
who filled the office for two years
and knows whereof he speaks, said
| Saturday: Mac Pugh makes a


clean Is cents profit each day on
the cost of boarding each of the
! county prisoners. The county


could buy the grub and give the
| prisoners more and better food for
20 cents per day. That is the plan
followed in King county, where the


cost of feeding prisoners under
Sheriff Hodge has been reduced to
almost what it is in Spokane coun-
! ty.
"Under the present plan, and
with the police working to keep the
| jail full, the feeding of prisoners is
! easily worth $4,000 net to the sher-
! iff every year."
